content.text: AWS CLI
The
AWS Command Line Interface (CLI)

is the primary tool for scripting and automating AWS operations. Where the console requires clicking through forms, the CLI accepts structured commands that can be composed into shell scripts, CI pipelines, and cron jobs. Every action you can perform in the console maps to a CLI command — and the CLI exposes a few things the console does not, such as filtering output with JMESPath queries and chaining commands with shell pipes.

The AWS CLI v2 is the current major version. It requires no separate Python installation (it ships as a self-contained binary), supports AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) natively, and adds YAML output and auto-prompting features over v1. All code examples in this document use CLI v2 syntax and behavior.

attrs.alt: AWS CLI architecture and command flow diagram
attrs.title: AWS CLI — Architecture and Command Flow

content.text: Installing the CLI

AWS provides official installers for Linux, macOS, and Windows. The recommended installation method on Linux and macOS is the bundled installer (not pip), which avoids conflicts with system Python packages and ensures the CLI binary is self-contained.

# Linux (x86_64) — official bundled installer
curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"
unzip awscliv2.zip
sudo ./aws/install
aws --version   # → aws-cli/2.x.x Python/3.x ...

# macOS — via the official pkg installer or Homebrew
brew install awscli
aws --version

# Windows — download the MSI installer:
# https://awscli.amazonaws.com/AWSCLIV2.msi
# Then: aws --version  (in PowerShell or CMD)

# Verify installation
aws --version
# Example output: aws-cli/2.15.0 Python/3.11.6 Linux/6.1.0 botocore/2.0.0
Configuring Credentials
The CLI needs credentials to sign requests to the AWS API. The most common setup for local development is
named profiles

stored in `~/.aws/credentials` and `~/.aws/config`. Running `aws configure` is the interactive way to create the default profile — it prompts for your access key ID, secret access key, default region, and output format.

# Interactive setup — creates [default] profile
aws configure
# AWS Access Key ID [None]: AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
# AWS Secret Access Key [None]: wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY
# Default region name [None]: us-east-1
# Default output format [None]: json

# Or configure a named profile (not overwriting the default)
aws configure --profile mycompany-dev
# → creates [mycompany-dev] in ~/.aws/credentials and ~/.aws/config

# The resulting ~/.aws/credentials file:
# [default]
# aws_access_key_id = AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
# aws_secret_access_key = wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY
#
# [mycompany-dev]
# aws_access_key_id = AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE2
# aws_secret_access_key = ...

# The resulting ~/.aws/config file:
# [default]
# region = us-east-1
# output = json
#
# [profile mycompany-dev]
# region = eu-west-1
# output = yaml
IAM Identity Center (SSO) Authentication

For organizations using IAM Identity Center, long-lived access keys are replaced by short-lived tokens obtained through the SSO login flow. This is the preferred authentication method — credentials automatically expire (typically after 8–12 hours), reducing the risk of key compromise. To configure SSO-based CLI access, you add the `sso_*` settings to a named profile in `~/.aws/config`, then run `aws sso login --profile <name>` to authenticate through a browser.

# ~/.aws/config — SSO-backed profile
# [profile mycompany-prod]
# sso_start_url = https://mycompany.awsapps.com/start
# sso_region = us-east-1
# sso_account_id = 123456789012
# sso_role_name = DevOpsEngineer
# region = us-east-1
# output = json

# Authenticate (opens browser for SSO login):
aws sso login --profile mycompany-prod

# Use the profile:
aws s3 ls --profile mycompany-prod

# Check which identity the CLI is currently using:
aws sts get-caller-identity --profile mycompany-prod
# {
#   "UserId": "AROAEXAMPLEID:alice@mycompany.com",
#   "Account": "123456789012",
#   "Arn": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:assumed-role/DevOpsEngineer/alice@mycompany.com"
# }
CLI Command Structure

Every AWS CLI command follows the same structural pattern: `aws <service> <operation> [--parameter value ...]`. The service name corresponds to the AWS service (such as `ec2`, `s3`, `rds`, `iam`, `lambda`). The operation is the API action within that service (such as `describe-instances`, `create-bucket`, `list-functions`). Parameters are passed as `--flag value` pairs.

# Pattern: aws <service> <operation> [--param value ...]

# List EC2 instances
aws ec2 describe-instances

# Create an S3 bucket
aws s3api create-bucket   --bucket my-unique-bucket-name   --region us-east-1

# Get a Lambda function's configuration
aws lambda get-function-configuration   --function-name my-function

# Invoke a Lambda function synchronously
aws lambda invoke   --function-name my-function   --payload '{"key": "value"}'   --cli-binary-format raw-in-base64-out   /tmp/response.json
cat /tmp/response.json

# Describe IAM roles
aws iam list-roles --query "Roles[*].{Name:RoleName,Arn:Arn}" --output table
Pagination

Many AWS list operations return paginated results — they return a subset of resources plus a `NextToken` that marks where the next page begins. The CLI handles this automatically with the `--no-paginate` / `--paginate` behavior: by default, `aws ec2 describe-instances` automatically fetches all pages and returns the combined result. This is convenient for small result sets but can be slow and memory-intensive for thousands of resources. Use `--page-size` to control how many items each API call fetches (affects network latency but not the total results returned) and `--max-items` to cap the total number of results returned.

# Default: CLI auto-paginates — fetches all pages automatically
aws ec2 describe-instances

# Fetch 10 items per API call (reduces memory pressure, same total output)
aws ec2 describe-instances --page-size 10

# Return only the first 5 items total (stops fetching after that)
aws ec2 describe-instances --max-items 5

# Manual pagination: use --starting-token with the NextToken value
aws ec2 describe-instances --max-items 5   --starting-token eyJNYXJrZXIiOiBudWxsLCAiYm90b190cnVuY2F0ZV9hbW91...

# Disable auto-pagination (returns first page + NextToken, your code pages through)
aws ec2 describe-instances --no-paginate
Filtering Output with --query and --filter
Raw CLI output for describe operations can be hundreds of lines of JSON. Two mechanisms let you extract exactly what you need:
`--query`
(a JMESPath expression applied to the JSON response after the API call) and
`--filter`

(server-side filtering applied before the API returns results, reducing the data transferred). Using `--filter` is more efficient when you know the filter criteria — `--query` is always applied locally on whatever the API returned.

# --query: JMESPath applied client-side to JSON response
# Get just the InstanceId and State for all instances
aws ec2 describe-instances   --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].{ID:InstanceId,State:State.Name,Type:InstanceType}"   --output table

# Get only running instances IDs as a flat list
aws ec2 describe-instances   --query "Reservations[*].Instances[?State.Name=='running'].InstanceId"   --output text

# --filters: server-side filtering (more efficient for large accounts)
aws ec2 describe-instances   --filters "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running"              "Name=tag:Environment,Values=production"   --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].InstanceId"   --output text

# Combine both for precise, efficient results:
# Find running t3.micro instances tagged production in a specific VPC
aws ec2 describe-instances   --filters     "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running"     "Name=instance-type,Values=t3.micro"     "Name=tag:Environment,Values=production"   --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].{ID:InstanceId,AZ:Placement.AvailabilityZone}"   --output table
Working with S3 from the CLI

The AWS CLI provides two S3 command families: `aws s3` (high-level sync/copy operations with recursive support) and `aws s3api` (direct API calls for fine-grained control). The high-level `s3` commands are more intuitive for common file operations; the `s3api` commands are needed for operations like setting bucket policies, enabling versioning, or working with object metadata.

# High-level s3 commands
aws s3 ls                                  # list all buckets
aws s3 ls s3://my-bucket/prefix/           # list objects under prefix
aws s3 cp localfile.txt s3://my-bucket/    # upload a file
aws s3 cp s3://my-bucket/key.txt ./        # download a file
aws s3 sync ./local-dir/ s3://my-bucket/  # sync directory to bucket
aws s3 sync s3://my-bucket/ ./backup/     # sync bucket to local directory
aws s3 rm s3://my-bucket/old-file.txt     # delete an object
aws s3 mb s3://new-bucket-name            # create a bucket

# Sync with exclusions and storage class override
aws s3 sync ./dist/ s3://my-bucket/   --exclude "*.DS_Store"   --storage-class INTELLIGENT_TIERING   --delete  # removes objects in S3 not present locally

# Low-level s3api for precise control
aws s3api put-bucket-versioning   --bucket my-bucket   --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled

aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket my-bucket | python3 -m json.tool

# Upload with server-side encryption
aws s3api put-object   --bucket my-bucket   --key sensitive/data.json   --body data.json   --server-side-encryption aws:kms   --ssekms-key-id alias/my-key
Environment Variables and Profile Switching

Environment variables take precedence over the `~/.aws/credentials` file in the credential resolution chain. This makes them useful in CI/CD pipelines where credentials are injected as environment variables by the pipeline runner (GitHub Actions secrets, CodeBuild environment variables, etc.). The key environment variables are `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN` (for temporary credentials from STS), `AWS_DEFAULT_REGION`, and `AWS_PROFILE` (to select a named profile without using `--profile` on every command).

# Override credentials via environment variables (useful in CI/CD)
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION="us-east-1"

# All subsequent aws commands use these environment credentials
aws sts get-caller-identity

# Switch profiles without --profile flag on every command
export AWS_PROFILE="mycompany-prod"
aws s3 ls   # uses mycompany-prod credentials

# Temporary credentials from STS AssumeRole
CREDS=$(aws sts assume-role   --role-arn "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/DeployRole"   --role-session-name "deploy-$(date +%s)"   --query "Credentials" --output json)

export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$(echo $CREDS | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['AccessKeyId'])")
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$(echo $CREDS | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['SecretAccessKey'])")
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=$(echo $CREDS | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['SessionToken'])")

# Now operating as the assumed role
aws sts get-caller-identity
CLI in Scripts and CI/CD Pipelines
The AWS CLI is designed to work in unattended script environments. When writing CLI scripts, a few patterns make the difference between fragile one-liners and robust automation:
Check exit codes
: every CLI command exits 0 on success and non-zero on failure. Use `set -e` in bash scripts to stop immediately on failure, or check `$?` explicitly.
Use --output text for simple values
: `--output text` with a `--query` that selects a single scalar returns the raw value without quotes — easier to assign to shell variables than parsing JSON.
Avoid parsing JSON with sed/awk
: use `jq` or Python's `json` module for reliable JSON parsing in scripts.
Use --dry-run for destructive operations
: many EC2 operations support `--dry-run` to validate permissions without executing — use it to test IAM policies before applying.
Capture and log output
: redirect stdout and stderr to log files in pipeline scripts for debugging failed runs.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail  # exit on error, unset vars, pipe failures

# Get the account ID cleanly
ACCOUNT_ID=$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)
REGION="${AWS_DEFAULT_REGION:-us-east-1}"

# Wait for an EC2 instance to reach running state
INSTANCE_ID="i-0abc123def456"
echo "Waiting for $INSTANCE_ID to be running..."
aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids "$INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Instance $INSTANCE_ID is running"

# Dry-run to verify permissions before the real operation
aws ec2 run-instances   --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0   --instance-type t3.micro   --dry-run 2>&1 | grep -q "DryRunOperation" && echo "Permissions OK"

# Deploy a CloudFormation stack and wait for completion
aws cloudformation deploy   --template-file infra/main.yaml   --stack-name "myapp-${ENVIRONMENT}"   --parameter-overrides Environment="$ENVIRONMENT" Version="$VERSION"   --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM   --no-fail-on-empty-changeset

# Get stack output value
API_URL=$(aws cloudformation describe-stacks   --stack-name "myapp-${ENVIRONMENT}"   --query "Stacks[0].Outputs[?OutputKey=='ApiUrl'].OutputValue"   --output text)
echo "API deployed at: $API_URL"
Useful CLI Patterns for Common Tasks
Task
CLI Command
List all EC2 instances with Name tag
aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].{ID:InstanceId,Name:Tags[?Key==`Name`]|[0].Value}' --output table
Find public S3 buckets
aws s3api list-buckets --query 'Buckets[*].Name' --output text | xargs -I{} aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket {}
Get Lambda logs from CloudWatch
aws logs tail /aws/lambda/my-function --follow
Describe recent CloudTrail events
aws cloudtrail lookup-events --max-results 20 --query 'Events[*].{User:Username,Event:EventName,Time:EventTime}'
List IAM users without MFA
aws iam generate-credential-report && aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | grep ,false,
Get RDS endpoint for a DB
aws rds describe-db-instances --db-instance-identifier mydb --query 'DBInstances[0].Endpoint.{Host:Address,Port:Port}'
Find unused Elastic IPs
aws ec2 describe-addresses --query 'Addresses[?AssociationId==null].{IP:PublicIp,AllocId:AllocationId}'
Tail ECS service logs
aws logs tail /ecs/my-service --since 1h --follow
CLI Wait Commands

Many AWS operations are asynchronous — launching an EC2 instance, creating an RDS database, or deploying a CloudFormation stack takes time, and the CLI returns immediately after initiating the operation. The `aws <service> wait <condition>` subcommands poll the relevant API on a configurable interval (typically every 15 seconds) and exit 0 when the condition is met, or exit 255 after a maximum number of attempts (typically 40, giving a ~10 minute timeout). This is cleaner and more reliable than writing your own polling loop.

# Wait for EC2 instance to reach 'running' state
aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids i-0abc123def456

# Wait for EC2 instance to pass both status checks (2/2)
aws ec2 wait instance-status-ok --instance-ids i-0abc123def456

# Wait for RDS instance to become available
aws rds wait db-instance-available --db-instance-identifier my-database

# Wait for CloudFormation stack update to complete
aws cloudformation wait stack-update-complete --stack-name my-stack

# Wait for an ECS service to reach steady state (all tasks running)
aws ecs wait services-stable   --cluster my-cluster   --services my-service

# Wait for an S3 object to exist (useful after triggering a process that creates it)
aws s3api wait object-exists   --bucket my-bucket   --key output/result.json
CLI Configuration File Options

Beyond credentials, the `~/.aws/config` file supports many settings that tune CLI behavior per profile. The `cli_pager` setting controls how long outputs are displayed — setting it to an empty string disables the pager (useful in scripts where paging causes the command to hang waiting for keyboard input). The `output` setting sets the default format. The `s3` section controls multipart upload thresholds and concurrency. Role assumption can be configured directly in a profile so that `--profile my-prod` automatically assumes a cross-account role without the caller needing to manage STS tokens manually.

# ~/.aws/config — advanced profile configuration example

[profile mycompany-dev]
region = us-east-1
output = json
cli_pager =                    # disable pager output (no less/more)

[profile mycompany-prod]
region = us-east-1
output = json
# Automatically assume a cross-account role when using this profile:
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::999988887777:role/DeployRole
source_profile = mycompany-dev   # use dev credentials to assume prod role
mfa_serial = arn:aws:iam::111122223333:mfa/alice  # prompt for MFA token
role_session_name = alice-prod-session
duration_seconds = 3600

[profile mycompany-prod]
s3 =
  max_concurrent_requests = 20
  multipart_threshold = 64MB
  multipart_chunksize = 16MB
  max_bandwidth = 50MB/s
Troubleshooting CLI Errors
'Unable to locate credentials'
: no credentials found in any provider. Run `aws configure` or set `AWS_PROFILE` to a valid profile, or attach an IAM role to the compute resource.
'An error occurred (AuthFailure)'
: credentials are present but invalid or expired. Refresh SSO tokens with `aws sso login`, or regenerate access keys in IAM.
'An error occurred (AccessDenied)'
: credentials are valid but the IAM principal lacks the required permission. Check the IAM policy attached to the user or role for the specific action and resource.
'Could not connect to the endpoint URL'
: network issue. Check VPC endpoints, proxy settings (`HTTPS_PROXY` env var), or firewall rules. Use `--debug` to see the exact URL being attempted.
'An error occurred (RequestExpired)'
: system clock is more than 5 minutes off from UTC. AWS SigV4 signing requires clock synchronization — run `chronyc makestep` or `ntpdate` to sync.
Slow operations with large accounts
: add `--page-size 50` to reduce API response sizes; add `--max-items` to limit output; use `--filters` to reduce server-side data before retrieval.

# Debug mode — prints raw HTTP requests, response headers, and retry logic
aws ec2 describe-instances --debug 2>&1 | head -50

# Check which profile and region the CLI will use
aws configure list
# Name                    Value             Type    Location
# ----                    -----             ----    --------
# profile                 mycompany-dev     env     ['--profile', 'AWS_PROFILE', ...]
# access_key              ****************WXYZ    shared-credentials-file
# secret_key              ****************/Bcd    shared-credentials-file
# region                  us-east-1         config-file    ~/.aws/config

# Verify current identity
aws sts get-caller-identity
Shell Auto-Completion

The CLI ships with a completion script that enables tab-completion of service names, operation names, and parameter flags in bash and zsh. Enabling completion dramatically reduces typing errors and the time spent referencing documentation for parameter names. With completion active, typing `aws ec2 describe-<TAB>` shows all operations starting with 'describe', and `aws s3api put-bucket-<TAB>` shows all `put-bucket-*` operations.

# Enable auto-completion in bash — add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.bash_profile
complete -C aws_completer aws
# Then source the file:
source ~/.bashrc

# Enable auto-completion in zsh — add to ~/.zshrc
autoload bashcompinit && bashcompinit
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit
complete -C aws_completer aws

# Verify aws_completer is in PATH (installed with CLI):
which aws_completer
# /usr/local/bin/aws_completer

# Test completion (press Tab after typing):
aws ec2 describe-<TAB>
# describe-account-attributes    describe-dhcp-options
# describe-addresses             describe-egress-only-internet-gateways
# describe-availability-zones    describe-elastic-gpus
# ... (full list of describe-* operations)

The auto-completer is context-aware: after typing `aws ec2 describe-instances --`, pressing Tab shows `--filters`, `--instance-ids`, `--query`, `--output`, and all other valid parameters for that specific operation. This eliminates the most common CLI error pattern — mistyping a parameter name — and makes exploratory CLI use much faster. For new service operators, tab completion is a practical learning tool: it reveals what options a command accepts without leaving the terminal.
CLI Aliases
The AWS CLI supports
aliases

— shorthand commands defined in `~/.aws/cli/alias` that map to longer CLI invocations or even shell scripts. Aliases reduce repetitive typing for frequently run commands and serve as a lightweight command library for a team. Unlike shell aliases (`alias`), CLI aliases live in a dedicated config file and are scoped to the `aws` command namespace, so they work consistently across bash, zsh, and PowerShell without shell-specific configuration.

# ~/.aws/cli/alias — CLI alias definitions

[toplevel]
# Shorthand for getting caller identity
whoami = sts get-caller-identity

# List all running EC2 instances across a region
running = ec2 describe-instances     --filters "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running"     --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].{ID:InstanceId,Name:Tags[?Key=='Name']|[0].Value,Type:InstanceType,AZ:Placement.AvailabilityZone}"     --output table

# Quick S3 bucket listing
buckets = s3 ls

# Get the current month's cost per service
monthlycost = ce get-cost-and-usage     --time-period Start=$(date +%Y-%m-01),End=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)     --granularity MONTHLY     --metrics BlendedCost     --group-by Type=DIMENSION,Key=SERVICE     --query "ResultsByTime[0].Groups[*].{Service:Keys[0],Cost:Metrics.BlendedCost.Amount}"     --output table
Processing CLI Output with jq
While `--query` (JMESPath) handles most filtering needs,
jq

is a more powerful tool for complex JSON transformations. jq is a standalone binary (not part of the AWS CLI) that is standard in most Linux distributions and available on macOS via Homebrew. The two tools complement each other: `--query` is ideal for selecting fields and filtering lists; `jq` is better for transformations, conditional logic, and generating custom output formats.

A common pattern is to use `--output json` with a minimal `--query` to select the relevant sub-tree, then pipe to `jq` for the final transformation. This avoids the verbosity of deeply nested JMESPath while keeping the initial `--query` to reduce the JSON volume being piped.

# jq examples with AWS CLI output

# Extract instance IDs as a bash array
INSTANCE_IDS=($(aws ec2 describe-instances   --filters "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running"   --output json   | jq -r '.Reservations[].Instances[].InstanceId'))

echo "Running instances: ${INSTANCE_IDS[@]}"

# Pretty-print a specific IAM policy document
aws iam get-policy-version   --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess   --version-id v1   --query 'PolicyVersion.Document'   --output json | jq .

# Build a CSV report of EBS volumes: ID, size, state, instance attached to
aws ec2 describe-volumes --output json   | jq -r '.Volumes[] | [.VolumeId, (.Size | tostring), .State, (.Attachments[0].InstanceId // "unattached")] | @csv'

# Find Lambda functions consuming more than 256MB memory
aws lambda list-functions --output json   | jq '.Functions[] | select(.MemorySize > 256) | {Name: .FunctionName, Memory: .MemorySize, Runtime: .Runtime}'

# Count resources by type using group-by
aws ec2 describe-instances --output json   | jq '[.Reservations[].Instances[].InstanceType] | group_by(.) | map({type: .[0], count: length}) | sort_by(.count) | reverse[]'
# → {type: "t3.medium", count: 12}
#   {type: "t3.micro", count: 8}
#   ...
Security Considerations for CLI Usage

The CLI is a powerful tool precisely because it bypasses the guards that make the console slow (confirmation dialogs, multi-step wizards, review pages). This power requires discipline: a wrong `aws ec2 terminate-instances` or `aws s3 rm s3://bucket --recursive` cannot be undone. Before running destructive CLI operations in production, verify the target by running the equivalent `describe` command and confirming the resource IDs, then use `--dry-run` where available.
Access key hygiene

is the most critical security practice for CLI users. Access keys are long-lived credentials that, if leaked (committed to a Git repository, stored in a log file, shared via Slack), provide API access until manually rotated. AWS
IAM Access Analyzer
and
GitHub secret scanning

can detect committed access keys, but detection after the fact is too late — the keys must be rotated immediately and audit logs checked for unauthorized use. Prefer IAM Identity Center (short-lived tokens) over IAM access keys wherever possible.
When access keys must be used (for example, a service account in a non-AWS CI system), follow these practices: set the
minimum permissions
the key needs via a tightly scoped IAM policy,
rotate the keys every 90 days
(enforced by Trusted Advisor and IAM Access Analyzer), store them in the CI system's secret manager (GitHub Actions Secrets, GitLab CI/CD Variables) and never in code or `.env` files, and enable
CloudTrail

so all API calls made with the key are logged. For CLI use on EC2 instances, never generate access keys — attach an IAM instance profile instead, which provides temporary credentials automatically rotated by AWS.

# Audit access key age — find keys older than 90 days
aws iam generate-credential-report
sleep 5
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text   | base64 -d   | awk -F',' 'NR>1 && $9!="N/A" { print $1, $9 }'
# Outputs: username, access_key_1_last_rotated_date

# Rotate an access key:
# 1. Create a new key
NEW_KEY=$(aws iam create-access-key --user-name alice)
echo $NEW_KEY | python3 -m json.tool
# 2. Update applications to use the new key
# 3. Delete the old key
aws iam delete-access-key --user-name alice --access-key-id AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE

# List all access keys and their status
aws iam list-access-keys --user-name alice   --query 'AccessKeyMetadata[*].{ID:AccessKeyId,Status:Status,Created:CreateDate}'

# Check if CLI is using instance profile (preferred for EC2)
aws sts get-caller-identity
# Arn should contain "assumed-role" if using an instance profile, not "user/"
Key Takeaways
The AWS CLI v2 is the current version — install via the official bundled installer, not pip.
Command structure: `aws <service> <operation> [--param value ...]`; all console operations have a CLI equivalent.
Credentials resolve in order: CLI flags → environment variables → `~/.aws/credentials` → container role → EC2 instance profile → SSO cache.
Use `--query` (JMESPath) for client-side output filtering and `--filters` for server-side filtering; combine them for efficient, precise results.
Configure IAM Identity Center (SSO) profiles with `sso_*` settings and authenticate via `aws sso login` — avoids long-lived access keys.
`aws <service> wait <condition>` commands poll asynchronous operations cleanly without manual sleep loops.
In CI/CD, inject credentials via environment variables; never hardcode access keys in scripts or commit them to version control.
